source_paper_title: On the Completeness of Several Fortification-Interdiction Games in the Polynomial Hierarchy
context:
Section 6 (Conclusion), page 19

Alternatively, if one of the lowest level sets of weights and profits becomes unitary (so that the lowest level KP becomes polynomial), what is the complexity of the problem when at least one set of higher level costs is instead arbitrary? Such clarifications may help to understand the articulation of the different levels which defines the intrinsic complexity of higher level problems in the polynomial hierarchy.
title: Complexity of multi-level interdiction knapsack when the lower-level knapsack is polynomial but higher-level costs are arbitrary
background_latex:
Consider a multi-level interdiction--fortification knapsack game over a finite item set \(I\). Each item \(i\in I\) has a profit \(p_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\) and a weight \(w_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\). The lowest (follower) level is a 0--1 knapsack with capacity \(W\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\): it selects \(I_1\subseteq I\) with \(\sum_{i\in I_1} w_i\le W\) to optimize a profit criterion (as in the paper's multi-level interdiction knapsack variants).

Above the knapsack level there are \(m\ge 1\) additional decision rounds that alternate between interdiction (attacker) and fortification (defender). For each higher level \(\ell\in\{2,\dots,m+1\}\), there is a budget \(B_\ell\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\). In the unit-cost version, each interdiction or fortification consumes 1 unit of budget per chosen item, i.e., \(|I_\ell|\le B_\ell\), where \(I_\ell\subseteq I\) is the set interdicted or fortified at level \(\ell\). In a non-unit-cost version, each item \(i\) can have an arbitrary interdiction cost and/or fortification cost at higher levels, so the level-\(\ell\) feasibility is expressed by a knapsack-type constraint \(\sum_{i\in I_\ell} c_{\ell i}\le B_\ell\) for given costs \(c_{\ell i}\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\).

Fortification is persistent and prevents interdiction: if an item is fortified in some level, it cannot be interdicted in later levels; interdicted items are unavailable to the final knapsack choice. The overall decision problem asks whether the top-level player has a strategy so that, against all moves of the opposing player(s), the final knapsack value meets (or fails to meet) a specified goal \(\bar K\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\), yielding a quantified formula of the form \(\exists I_{m+1}\,\forall I_m\,\exists I_{m-1}\cdots \exists I_1\) (depending on parity).

Assume now that the lowest-level knapsack becomes polynomially solvable because one of its parameter sets is unitary: either \(w_i=1\) for all \(i\in I\) (unit weights) or \(p_i=1\) for all \(i\in I\) (unit profits).
problem_definition_latex: Problem: Determine the computational complexity (in terms of membership and hardness in standard complexity classes, e.g. the polynomial hierarchy) of the above multi-level interdiction--fortification knapsack decision problem in the regime where the lowest-level knapsack is polynomially solvable (i.e., \(w_i=1\ \forall i\in I\) or \(p_i=1\ \forall i\in I\)), but at least one set of higher-level interdiction and/or fortification costs \(c_{\ell i}\) (for some \(\ell\ge 2\)) is arbitrary (non-unit), subject to the corresponding budgets \(B_\ell\).
latex:
Consider a multi-level interdiction--fortification knapsack game over a finite item set \(I\). Each item \(i\in I\) has a profit \(p_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\) and a weight \(w_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\). The lowest (follower) level is a 0--1 knapsack with capacity \(W\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\): it selects \(I_1\subseteq I\) with \(\sum_{i\in I_1} w_i\le W\) to optimize a profit criterion (as in the paper's multi-level interdiction knapsack variants).

Above the knapsack level there are \(m\ge 1\) additional decision rounds that alternate between interdiction (attacker) and fortification (defender). For each higher level \(\ell\in\{2,\dots,m+1\}\), there is a budget \(B_\ell\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\). In the unit-cost version, each interdiction or fortification consumes 1 unit of budget per chosen item, i.e., \(|I_\ell|\le B_\ell\), where \(I_\ell\subseteq I\) is the set interdicted or fortified at level \(\ell\). In a non-unit-cost version, each item \(i\) can have an arbitrary interdiction cost and/or fortification cost at higher levels, so the level-\(\ell\) feasibility is expressed by a knapsack-type constraint \(\sum_{i\in I_\ell} c_{\ell i}\le B_\ell\) for given costs \(c_{\ell i}\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\).

Fortification is persistent and prevents interdiction: if an item is fortified in some level, it cannot be interdicted in later levels; interdicted items are unavailable to the final knapsack choice. The overall decision problem asks whether the top-level player has a strategy so that, against all moves of the opposing player(s), the final knapsack value meets (or fails to meet) a specified goal \(\bar K\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\), yielding a quantified formula of the form \(\exists I_{m+1}\,\forall I_m\,\exists I_{m-1}\cdots \exists I_1\) (depending on parity).

Assume now that the lowest-level knapsack becomes polynomially solvable because one of its parameter sets is unitary: either \(w_i=1\) for all \(i\in I\) (unit weights) or \(p_i=1\) for all \(i\in I\) (unit profits).

Problem:
Problem: Determine the computational complexity (in terms of membership and hardness in standard complexity classes, e.g. the polynomial hierarchy) of the above multi-level interdiction--fortification knapsack decision problem in the regime where the lowest-level knapsack is polynomially solvable (i.e., \(w_i=1\ \forall i\in I\) or \(p_i=1\ \forall i\in I\)), but at least one set of higher-level interdiction and/or fortification costs \(c_{\ell i}\) (for some \(\ell\ge 2\)) is arbitrary (non-unit), subject to the corresponding budgets \(B_\ell\).
open_problem_quote: Alternatively, if one of the lowest level sets of weights and profits becomes unitary (so that the lowest level KP becomes polynomial), what is the complexity of the problem when at least one set of higher level costs is instead arbitrary? Such clarifications may help to understand the articulation of the different levels which defines the intrinsic complexity of higher level problems in the polynomial hierarchy.
subject_classification: Computational Complexity in Combinatorial Optimization (Polynomial Hierarchy; Multi-level/Bilevel Optimization)
